topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crowded coffee shop buzzed with a low, chaotic energy, but my focus narrowed immediately onto the man across the small table. He was laughing, a deep, resonant sound that made my chest tighten. I’d been watching him for nearly ten minutes, a strange, unsettling certainty blooming in my gut. Then, he turned, and his eyes—those exact, startlingly blue eyes—met mine. A jolt, sharp and unwelcome, shot up my arm. He smiled, a slow, knowing curve of his lips, and in that instant, the world tilted. I mumbled an apology for staring, my voice sounding thin and reedy, and retreated to the edge of the booth, my hands gripping my mug so tightly my knuckles were white. It felt like a trap had sprung shut around me.

[story 2]

I was waiting for the train, the platform a blur of hurried commuters and echoing announcements. When he appeared, leaning against a pillar, the air around him seemed to shift. He looked familiar, undeniably so, yet the recognition hit me with the force of a physical blow. My breath hitched, catching somewhere in my throat. When he finally approached, offering a casual greeting, my feet felt rooted to the cold concrete. I managed a weak nod, my gaze darting everywhere but his face, trying desperately to process the impossible confluence of shared history. Every muscle in my body was coiled tight, ready to bolt, but my feet remained stubbornly planted, paralyzed by the sheer absurdity of the situation.

[story 3]

The gallery opening was overwhelming—too many people, too much music, too much polished surface. Then I saw him. Standing near a sculpture bathed in harsh white light, he turned, and the recognition was immediate, a sickening drop in my stomach. He looked directly at me, and the casual warmth in his expression curdled into something sharp and alarming. My palms began to sweat, slicking my hands against the cool glass of my drink. I took an involuntary step backward, bumping into a display stand, the sudden movement making the surrounding chatter seem distant and muffled. I could only stare, my mouth dry, as the realization settled: this person, this shared glance, this undeniable familiarity—it was all me.
